title: Reconvergence-driven cut — preferring shared internal nodes

steps.title: What this primitive picks at each step
steps.narrative:
Abc_NodeGetLeafCostOne (abcReconv.c:126) returns the number of brand-new leaves that would be added if the given leaf were popped and replaced by its two fanins:

cost(leaf) = (¬fanin0.in_cone_or_leaves) + (¬fanin1.in_cone_or_leaves)

So cost 0 = both fanins already in the cut → expansion shrinks |L| by 1. Cost 1 = one fanin shared → |L| unchanged. Cost 2 = neither shared → |L| grows. PIs return 999 (cannot expand).

Abc_NodeBuildCutLevelOne_int picks the leaf with the lowest cost, breaking ties by deepest level. That's the "prefer reconvergent" bias: a leaf whose fanins are already present in the cone is reconvergent, and gets a strictly lower cost than one whose fanins are fresh.

entries.Target: n4 — find a reconv-driven cut at n4

panes.body: Watch what happens at iter 2: once n2 has been absorbed into the cone, n1 is in the leaves — and that makes n3's cost drop to 1, while every other expandable candidate still costs 2.

steps.title: Initial state: L = {n2, n3}, cone = {n4}
steps.narrative: Seed the leaf set with n4's two fanins. fMarkB is set on every node currently in the cone or on a leaf — that's the predicate the cost function reads to test "already in".

panes.body: Both candidates cost 2 (no shared fanins yet). Tie. abcReconv.c:333 breaks ties by Level (deeper wins); n2 and n3 are at the same level — pick by iteration order. Say n2.

steps.title: Iter 1: pop n2, push its fanins (n1 and c)
steps.narrative: Pop n2 from the leaves; absorb it into the cone. Push n2's fanins as new leaves: n1 and c. Set fMarkB = 1 on both. |L| went 2 → 3 (cost 2 = 2 new leaves – 1 popped + 1 each = +1).

steps.title: Iter 2: cost re-evaluation — reconvergence at n3!
steps.narrative:
Recompute every leaf's cost. n3's fanin n1 is now marked (it was added as a leaf in iter 1). So n3's cost drops to 1, beating every other candidate.

This is the reconvergence preference, made visible: among three leaves, one of them already shares structure with the cone and gets a strictly lower cost.

entries.Why: n1 is shared with the cone — only d is a fresh leaf

steps.title: Iter 2 expand: pop n3, push only d (n1 is already a leaf)
steps.narrative:
Pop n3 from leaves. Of n3's fanins, n1 is already in fMarkB — the push-only-if-not-marked check at abcReconv.c:357..362 skips it. Only d gets added.

|L| stays at 3. Without reconvergence (had we expanded n1 instead at cost 2), |L| would have grown to 4.

panes.body: Counterfactual: if we had expanded n1 at iter 2 instead (cost 2), L would have become {a, b, c, n3} — same size 4 — and we'd still need another iteration to absorb n3. The reconvergence path saves one round of cone growth.

steps.title: Iter 3: only n1 is expandable
steps.narrative: Of the three leaves, c and d are PIs (cost 999). Only n1 can expand. Cost 2 → expand. Push fanins a and b.

entries.Leaves: { a, b, c, d } — all PIs

steps.title: Final cut
steps.narrative: Refactor and resub now operate on this single cut: leaves {a, b, c, d}, cone {n4, n2, n3, n1}. The reconvergence-driven growth picked an order that avoided unnecessary leaf-set growth — saving |L| relative to a naïve top-down DFS that would expand the lowest-id leaf first regardless of cost.

panes.title: Where reconvergence really pays off
panes.body: On bigger circuits (resub default nCutMax = 8), the reconvergence preference is what keeps the cut from blowing past the leaf cap. A non-reconvergent expansion order on a moderately wide cone would hit |L| = 8 and bail before reaching meaningful internal divisors; reconv-driven order keeps |L| in check and lets the algorithm finish.
